annotations.text: The most compelling and likely diagnosis, given the totality of the clinical and radiologic findings, is **Wilson disease with late-onset, atypical presentation**.

the absence of liver disease makes this less likely. Another possibility is a disorder of copper metabolism, such as Wilson disease, which can present with basal ganglia lesions, psychiatric symptoms, and cognitive decline.

However, in patients with chronic liver disease or biliary obstruction, manganese accumulates due to impaired excretion; in the absence of liver dysfunction, one must consider whether there is an alternative mechanism of manganese retention—such as altered blood-brain barrier permeability, chronic inflammation, or an underlying hematologic disorder.

the patient’s chronic anemia raises the possibility of iron metabolism disorders, which may have downstream effects on manganese homeostasis, as both metals share transport pathways via divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) and ferroportin.
If these were low ceruloplasmin and elevated urinary copper, Wilson disease would be confirmed.

The differential diagnosis must be approached systematically, considering malignant, metabolic, autoimmune, neurodegenerative, and rare toxic causes, while rigorously excluding common mimics such as infectious encephalitis, vascular events, or structural lesions.

The symmetric basal ganglia hypointensity on susceptibility MRI, the progressive cognitive and behavioral decline over two years, the absence of infectious or neoplastic etiologies, the lack of response to antibiotics, and the normal liver function tests (which can be misleading in early or compensated Wilson disease) all point toward this diagnosis.
